title: Goya, The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (quiz)
topic_path: Goya, The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (quiz)

assessment_items.question_content: ***Los Caprichos* can be translated to mean [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
hints: Goya wrote that he chose his subjects for the series “from the multitude of follies and blunders common in every civil society, as well as from the vulgar prejudices and lies authorized by custom, ignorance or interest, those that he has thought most suitable matter for ridicule.”

Los Caprichos means caprices or follies.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-spain/a/goya-the-sleep-of-reason-produces-monsters)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**What printing technique used in "The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters" allowed for the rich and varied tones of the background?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/71acf6dab895da3c620588e03de719f7.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Aquatint is a variation on the etching technique of printmaking that allows for a more varied tonal surface.

Goya employed aquatint in *Los Caprichos* to create the varied tones of the background.  He fully exploits the atmospheric effects of the medium to create this fantastical scene.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-spain/a/goya-the-sleep-of-reason-produces-monsters)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the primary function of Goya's *Los Caprichos* series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Many of the prints in the *Caprichos* series express disdain for the pre-Enlightenment practices still popular in Spain at the end of the Eighteenth century (a powerful clergy, arranged marriages, superstition, etc.)

Los Caprichos were different from his earlier work in their biting critique of contemporary Spanish society.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-spain/a/goya-the-sleep-of-reason-produces-monsters)**

choices.content: to critique contemporary society in Spain
to promote the social reforms of King Carlos III
to advance ideals of the Enlightenment
to document the atrocities of the Napoleonic Guard

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which detail in "The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters" suggests Goya's interest in Romanticism?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/71acf6dab895da3c620588e03de719f7.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The title of the print, emblazoned on the front of the desk, is often read as a proclamation of Goya’s adherence to the values of the Enlightenment—without Reason, evil and corruption prevail.

In “The Sleep of Reason,"  Goya's reference to the imaginary dream world suggest that we should not be governed by reason alone—an idea central to Romanticism’s reaction against Enlightenment doctrine.

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-spain/a/goya-the-sleep-of-reason-produces-monsters)**

choices.content: the title emblazoned on the desk
the self-portrait of the artist asleep
the reference to dreams and imagination
